questions.question: A 300 kV portable X-ray exposure of a steel step block fails to put enough density under the heaviest section. To increase the beam's penetrating ability, the operator should raise:
options: The milliamperage, which increases the energy of each photon
The exposure time, which lets each photon travel farther into the steel
The tube kilovoltage, which raises the maximum photon energy of the continuous spectrum
The filament temperature, which accelerates the electrons to higher speeds
questions.explanation: Kilovoltage sets the accelerating potential and therefore the maximum photon energy — penetrating ability — while milliamperage × time sets only the quantity of radiation. More mA or more time darkens the film everywhere but cannot push photons through a section the spectrum cannot penetrate; the filament merely supplies electrons for the kV to accelerate. The standing trade-off: raising kV lowers subject contrast.

questions.question: A 150 mm (6 in.) thick steel valve body must be radiographed at a remote site with no electrical power. The appropriate source is:
options: Selenium-75
Cobalt-60
Iridium-192
A 300 kV portable X-ray unit
questions.explanation: Co-60's 1.17 and 1.33 MeV photons cover roughly 50–200 mm of steel, matching the 150 mm wall. Ir-192 tops out near 65 mm and Se-75 near 30 mm, and any X-ray machine needs the electrical power the site lacks — a 300 kV portable only reaches about 75 mm anyway. The price of Co-60 is heavy shielding, and like every isotope it can never be switched off.

questions.question: Cranking a gamma source back after an exposure, a radiographer's GM survey meter reads near zero as he approaches the exposure device. Before trusting that reading, he should remember that:
options: GM counters respond only to neutron radiation, not gamma rays
A near-zero reading is positive confirmation that the source is fully shielded
GM counters always read high, so the true field is even lower than indicated
A GM tube in a very intense radiation field can saturate and read low or near zero
questions.explanation: GM saturation is a potentially lethal failure mode: a single discharge per event makes the tube extremely sensitive, but in a very intense field it can choke and indicate almost nothing. The ion chamber is the radiographer's standard instrument for measuring exposure rate, and the iron rule of gamma work stands — survey the device, guide tube, and full crank-back path after every single exposure, because only the meter proves the source is home.

questions.question: A gamma technique on a 1.5 in. thick weld uses a source 0.2 in. in physical size, a flaw-to-detector distance of 1 in., and a source-to-object distance of 10 in. The geometric unsharpness is:
options: 0.020 in. — exactly at the ASME Section V limit for sections under 2 in.
0.002 in. — far inside the ASME Section V limit
0.050 in. — over the limit, so the setup must change
2.0 in. — over the limit by two orders of magnitude
questions.explanation: Ug = F·d/D = (0.2 × 1)/10 = 0.020 in., right at the ASME Section V cap for sections under 2 in. The formula points to the remedies: a longer source-to-object distance D, a smaller source F, or a detector held tighter against the part (smaller d). Swapping d and D is the classic error — it produces the absurd 2.0 in. answer.

questions.question: A weld radiograph shows the hole-type IQI plaque outline and the 2T hole clearly. Achieving this 2-2T quality level demonstrates that:
options: Every discontinuity larger than the 2T hole has been detected
The film density is within the code window of 1.8 to 4.0
Geometric unsharpness did not exceed 0.020 in.
The radiographic system achieved roughly 2% thickness sensitivity on this exposure
questions.explanation: The hole-type plaque is 2% of the section thickness, so rendering its outline plus the 2T-diameter hole proves the technique can show about a 2% change of section — system performance, demonstrated on the image itself. It is not a guarantee that every flaw of similar size is visible: a tight crack a few degrees off the beam axis can still hide. Density and unsharpness are verified separately, and the IQI normally rides the source side.

questions.question: On BOTH films of a double-loaded cassette, a weld radiograph shows a straight, sharply defined dark line tracking the weld centerline. The correct call is:
options: A crimp-mark artifact — reshoot the area
Incomplete penetration, a relevant discontinuity
A slag inclusion trapped along the bevel
A crater crack at the weld root
questions.explanation: Appearing on both films rules out an artifact, which usually prints on only one film of the pair. A ruler-straight dark line down the centerline is drawn by the machined, unfused root faces — the signature of incomplete penetration, and its geometric straightness is the giveaway. Slag prints irregular and off the centerline along the bevel; cracks wander, fade, and often branch.

questions.question: Hydrogen-rich materials such as water, polyethylene, and paraffin make the best neutron moderators because:
options: A neutron transfers the most energy in an elastic collision with a nucleus of nearly its own mass — and hydrogen's single proton is almost exactly that
Hydrogen's electron cloud slows passing neutrons by electrostatic drag
Hydrogen nuclei absorb fast neutrons and re-emit them at thermal energy
Hydrogenous materials are dense enough to stop neutrons by sheer mass
questions.explanation: Moderation is repeated elastic, billiard-ball scattering, and energy transfer peaks when the struck nucleus matches the neutron's mass — so MeV fission neutrons walk down to thermal energy (~0.025 eV, about 2200 m/s) in relatively few collisions with hydrogen. Neutrons carry no charge and ignore electron clouds entirely, and capture removes a neutron rather than slowing it.

questions.question: Personnel working around neutron radiography beams wear albedo TLDs or CR-39 track-etch badges because:
options: Neutron doses are exempt from the 10 CFR 20 occupational limits
Albedo TLDs are cheaper than film badges and need no processing
Standard beta-gamma dosimeters underrespond to neutrons, whose radiation weighting factor runs as high as about 20
CR-39 badges measure the beam's gamma contamination rather than neutron dose
questions.explanation: Neutrons deposit energy through recoil protons and capture products — dense ionization weighted at roughly 2.5 for thermal neutrons up to about 20 near 1 MeV, against 1 for X- and gamma rays — and a standard beta-gamma badge simply cannot see most of it. The dose-limit framework from RT applies unchanged; what changes is the dosimeter that can actually record the dose.

questions.question: A bismuth filter is placed at the collimator of a thermal-neutron beam because bismuth:
options: Captures thermal neutrons, hardening the beam for thicker objects
Scatters epithermal neutrons back into the moderator for reuse
Converts gamma rays into additional thermal neutrons by the (γ,n) reaction
Is nearly transparent to neutrons but opaque to photons, stripping gamma contamination that would fog direct-technique film
questions.explanation: Every real beam carries gamma contamination from capture reactions and the source itself, and in the direct technique the film sits in the beam where photons fog it. Bismuth is the rare heavy element with a tiny neutron cross section: neutrons pass, photons are absorbed. The (γ,n) reaction belongs to Sb-124/beryllium sources, not filters — and transfer-technique film never sees the beam at all, which is its own gamma defense.

questions.question: Gadolinium oxide is blended into the ceramic core material of hollow investment-cast turbine blades so that neutron radiography can:
options: Reveal even milligram traces of residual core in the cooling passages, since gadolinium's enormous capture cross section prints sharply through the near-transparent metal walls
Verify the nickel alloy reached full density during solidification
Measure the blade wall thickness written as film density
Detect surface-connected cracks in the blade root serrations
questions.explanation: Natural gadolinium has the highest thermal-neutron capture cross section of any natural element — about 49,000 barns — so a gadolinia tag turns even tiny leaching residues into strong absorbers that stand out through nickel walls neutrons barely notice. Metal density and wall thickness are photon-radiography questions, and surface cracks belong to PT; the blade goes to the reactor specifically for the light-element residue.
